guideline_references: eviQ / Cancer Council Australia — Pancreatic cancer suspected diagnosis pathway
NICE NG12 — Suspected cancer: recognition and referral (painless obstructive jaundice, unexplained weight loss)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who elicited Courvoisier's sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder), Virchow's node and hepatomegaly and arranged direct hospital admission for CT + biliary stenting. The AI Doctor CANNOT palpate the abdomen, feel a lymph node, take bloods or image, and is scored on recognising the sinister painless-jaundice-plus-weight-loss alarm pattern and arranging urgent same-day in-person/ED assessment + expedited imaging + urgent specialist referral — not on remotely confirming or excluding malignancy.

Alarm-feature recognition under false reassurance: the patient is afebrile and haemodynamically stable, and the jaundice is PAINLESS — the trap is attributing the itch to a skin problem, the jaundice to paracetamol or viral hepatitis, and under-triaging. Painless progressive obstructive jaundice with weight loss is malignancy until proven otherwise.
New-onset atypical diabetes 4 months ago in a non-obese patient with no family history is a paraneoplastic clue, not an incidental comorbidity — dismissing it is an error of omission.

T4/T5 ceiling discipline: unexcludable ascending cholangitis + pending bloods/imaging justify urgent same-day hospital assessment (T4), but there is NO patient-reportable time-critical emergency feature (no fever/rigors, no confusion, no haematemesis, no shock), so the correct action is urgent in-person assessment today, not a 000 ambulance — with a clear cholangitis escalation edge to T5.
Age (~68, older_adult) and sex (male) inferred — flagged; new diabetes at 4 months and cachexia (62 kg) recorded.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source note GIH-048.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
